Greetings.tip: Use it until early afternoon in most Spanish-speaking contexts.

Greetings.example_en: Hi, I am Ana. Nice to meet you.
Greetings.tip: A compact, natural way to greet someone politely.

Travel.example_en: Excuse me, where is the station?
Travel.tip: Dónde asks for location; está is used for where something is.

Travel.example_en: I need a ticket to Madrid.
Travel.tip: Boleto is common in many regions; billete may also be used.

Travel.english: Where can I buy a bus pass?
Travel.translation: ¿Dónde puedo comprar un pase de autobús?
Travel.pronunciation: DON-de PWE-do kom-PRAR oon PA-se de ow-to-BOOS
Travel.example_en: Where can I buy a bus pass for today?
Travel.example_translation: ¿Dónde puedo comprar un pase de autobús para hoy?
Travel.tip: Puedo + infinitive is useful when asking what you are able to do.

Food & Drink.example_en: I would like coffee with milk.
Food & Drink.tip: Quisiera sounds soft and polite when ordering.

Food & Drink.example_en: The check, please. Everything was delicious.
Food & Drink.example_translation: La cuenta, por favor. Todo estuvo delicioso.
Food & Drink.tip: La cuenta is what you ask for in a restaurant when you want the bill.

Numbers.tip: Spanish number words do not change for gender here.

Family.tip: Mi works for both masculine and feminine singular nouns.

Colors.tip: Many color words can stay unchanged across gender.

Work.example_en: I have a meeting at noon.

Time.tip: Hora is singular in this fixed expression.
